metadata.summary: A visual, card-based kanban workflow on Trello: board structure with WIP-limited lists, card anatomy with labels and checklists, Butler automation for rule-based transitions, custom fields for story points and sprint tracking, and REST API integration for agent-driven card management.

content.title: Trello Kanban
content.summary: A visual, card-based kanban workflow on Trello: board structure with WIP-limited lists, card anatomy with labels and checklists, Butler automation for rule-based transitions, custom fields for story points and sprint tracking, and REST API integration for agent-driven card management. WIP limits are enforced by list-name convention and Butler rules — not automatically blocked by the platform.

why.p: Trello's simplicity makes it the lowest-friction kanban tool for small teams and non-technical stakeholders. Its REST API is straightforward, and Butler automation handles mechanical state transitions (all checklists complete → move to Done) so agents only need to trigger higher-level operations. The tradeoff: no native velocity charts, no dependency tracking, no built-in OKR layer.

list.item: Team of 1–5 people needing a visual board with minimal setup overhead
Stakeholders are non-technical and need to update the board without training
Rapid prototyping or pre-MVP phases where flexibility beats structure
Budget constraints: Trello Free covers up to ~10 boards (1 Power-Up per board)
Team is already on GitHub — GitHub Projects has native code integration; use that instead
Complex dependency tracking needed — Trello has no native dependency visualization
OKR or goal tracking required — Trello has no goals layer; use ClickUp or Linear
Velocity metrics or burndown charts needed regularly — requires premium Power-Ups
More than ~10 boards under Free plan (Power-Up limit of 1 per board on Free)

rule.statement: Use 5–7 lists maximum. Encode WIP limits directly in list names: "In Progress (WIP: 3)". WIP is enforced by Butler rule or human review — Trello does not block card moves automatically.
rule.rationale: More than 7 lists create cognitive overhead. Named WIP limits make violations visible without requiring a Power-Up.

example.note: Board lists in order: Backlog → Ready for Dev → In Development (WIP: 3) → Code Review (WIP: 2) → QA Testing (WIP: 2) → Done. Archive done cards at sprint end — do not delete them (preserves velocity history).

rule.statement: Limit labels to 8–12 maximum. Assign labels by type (Feature, Bug, Tech Debt, Docs), priority (P0-Critical, P1-High, P2-Medium, P3-Low), and status modifiers (Blocked, Quick Win). Apply one type label per card — not Feature AND Bug simultaneously.
rule.rationale: Label bloat makes filtering unreliable. Agents producing cards without a fixed label set create arbitrary new labels that break board filters.

rule.statement: Every card must have: a clear action-oriented title, a description using the card template (user story, AC, technical notes, DoD checklist), at least one type label, a due date if committed for the current sprint, and an assignee.
rule.rationale: Unstructured card descriptions cannot be reliably parsed by agents. Templates at card creation enforce consistent structure.

rule.statement: Use Custom Fields Power-Up for story points (number), sprint (dropdown), and component (dropdown). These fields are readable/writable via the Trello REST API custom fields endpoint — use them instead of embedding data in card titles.
rule.rationale: Data in card titles cannot be queried or aggregated via the API. Custom fields are structured and filterable.

rule.statement: Archive the Done list at sprint end — do not delete cards. Create a new Done list for the next sprint. This preserves card history for velocity calculations via the API.
rule.rationale: Deleted cards cannot be retrieved for historical metrics. Archived cards are queryable via the Trello API archive endpoint.

rule.statement: Use Butler rules for mechanical state transitions: move card when all checklists are complete, add "Blocked" label and notify on manual block, post stale-card comment after 7 days of inactivity. Document all Butler rules in constitution.md — agents cannot create or modify Butler rules via API.
rule.rationale: Butler rules are UI-configured only; no API endpoint exists. Agents must treat Butler automation as pre-existing infrastructure.

example.note: Butler rule: when all checklists on a card are complete → move to Done. Butler rule: when card has no activity for 7 days AND is not in Done → add "Stale" label and post comment. These are configured once in the board UI and documented in constitution.md.

rule.statement: All write operations require board/list/card IDs (not names). Agents must do a read pass first to map human-readable names to IDs. Store board ID, list IDs, and label IDs in .aidocs/memory/ or constitution.md — do not fetch them on every session.
rule.rationale: Trello API requires numeric/hash IDs for all writes. Agents that skip the read pass fail silently or operate on wrong lists.

rule.statement: Respect the rate limit: 100 requests per 10 seconds per token. Parallel agent operations that create or move multiple cards simultaneously will hit this limit. Batch sequentially with a small delay or use n8n for bulk operations.
rule.rationale: Rate limit violations return 429 errors that agents must handle; unhandled, they cause silent data loss.

rule.statement: Moving a card to Done via API does not trigger Butler card-archive automation. If archiving is required, make a separate API call: PUT /cards/{id} with closed=true.
rule.rationale: Butler automation fires on UI events, not on API-triggered state changes. Agents that rely on Butler archive automation after an API move will leave cards unarchived.

antipattern.description: Creating a card with idList set by fetching the list name on every API call.
antipattern.reason: Wastes 1 API call per card write. Store list IDs in session memory after the first fetch.

rule.statement: Store TRELLO_KEY and TRELLO_TOKEN in environment variables or 1Password. Never hardcode in agent prompts, scripts, or configuration files.
rule.rationale: Trello tokens have board-level write access. Exposed tokens allow full board modification by anyone who reads the file.

template.purpose: Feature card template with user story, AC, technical notes, DoD checklist
Bug card template with steps to reproduce, severity, environment
